1	# State of the Art: Prime Computation (2024-2026)
2	
3	Consolidated survey of all developments relevant to computing p(n) exactly.
4	Deduplicated from 6 overlapping source files.
5	
6	Last updated: 2026-04-04
7	
8	---
9	
10	## 1. Prime Counting Algorithms
11	
12	### 1.1 Combinatorial Methods (Best Practical)
13	
14	**Current best asymptotic:** O(x^{2/3} / log^2 x) -- Deleglise-Rivat / Gourdon (1996/2001)
15	
16	**Best implementation:** Kim Walisch's primecount library
17	- Version 8.4 released 2026-04-01
18	- Implements Gourdon's algorithm with SIMD acceleration (AVX512, ARM SVE)
19	- primecount 8.3 (March 2026): bidirectional clustered easy leaves, faster compression
20	- primecount 8.1 (January 2026): 15% faster AC.cpp
21	- primecount 8.0 (December 2025): new 128-bit API
22	- pi(10^25) computed in 330 CPU core hours on AMD EPYC Zen4
23	- The SIMD-accelerated Gourdon D formula in v8.4 vectorizes the hard special leaves bottleneck, estimated 2-4x speedup on modern CPUs
24	
25	**Relevance to project:** This is the engine behind our best practical approach (binary search on pi(x)). Constant-factor improvements continue but no asymptotic breakthrough.
26	
27	**Does it change the barrier?** No. Still O(x^{2/3}).
28	
29	### 1.2 Analytic Methods (Best Theoretical)
30	
31	**Current best asymptotic:** O(x^{1/2+eps}) -- Lagarias-Odlyzko (1987)
32	
33	**Implementation status:**
34	- Platt's method: rigorous interval arithmetic, computed pi(10^25) unconditionally
35	- FKBJ method: Weil-Barner explicit formula with precomputed zeta zeros, 128-bit fixed-point
36	- In practice, 100x slower than combinatorial methods due to zeta zero computation overhead
37	- ~40,000 CPU core hours for pi(10^25) vs 330 for combinatorial
38	
39	**Relevance to project:** Theoretically better asymptotics but impractical constants. The 100x gap persists for all currently reachable values of x.
40	
41	**Does it change the barrier?** No. O(x^{1/2+eps}) has stood since 1987.
42	
43	### 1.3 Aggarwal: Complexity of Computing p(n) (October 2025)
44	
45	**Source:** arXiv:2510.16285
46	
47	**Key results:**
48	- p(n) computable in O(sqrt(n) * (log n)^4) unconditionally via binary search on pi(x)
49	- Conditional (RH + Cramer): O(sqrt(n) * (log n)^{7/2} * log log n)
50	- Proves sieve-based methods CANNOT asymptotically beat binary-search-on-pi(x)
51	  - Best sieve for p(n): O(n log n / log log n)
52	  - Binary search + pi(x): O(sqrt(n) * polylog(n))
53	  - Binary search wins by factor ~sqrt(n)
54	
55	**Relevance:** Most rigorous recent analysis. Confirms our implemented approach (Lucy_Hedgehog/Meissel-Lehmer + binary search) is provably superior to any sieve for computing a single p(n).
56	
57	**Does it change the barrier?** No new algorithm, but provides the tightest known complexity analysis.
58	
59	---
60	
61	## 2. Riemann Hypothesis and Zero-Density Estimates
62	
63	### 2.1 Guth-Maynard: Large Value Estimates (May 2024)
64	
65	**Source:** arXiv:2405.20552; published in Annals of Mathematics, 2025
66	
67	**The breakthrough:**
68	- First improvement to Ingham's 1940 zero-density bound in 80+ years
69	- Improved exponent from 3/5 = 0.6 to 13/25 = 0.52
70	- New zero-density estimate: N(sigma, T) <= T^{30(1-sigma)/13 + o(1)}
71	
72	**Propagated results:**
73	- PNT in short intervals: [x, x + x^{17/30}], improving from x^{3/5}
74	- Almost all short intervals: theta improved from 1/6 to 2/15
75	- Tao: "a remarkable achievement"; Radziwill: "first new idea in the hunt for zeta zeros in 50 years"
76	
77	**Relevance:** Does NOT directly yield a new algorithm, but tightens theoretical guarantees about prime distribution. Better zero-density bounds could reduce the number of zeta zeros needed for rigorous analytic pi(x) computation; practical impact unclear.
78	
79	**Does it change the barrier?** No. Improves density estimates, not computational methods.
80	
81	### 2.2 Gafni-Tao: Exceptional Intervals (May 2025)
82	
83	**Source:** arXiv:2505.24017
84	
85	Makes Guth-Maynard results explicit and quantitative for short intervals. Provides explicit upper bounds on exceptional set where PNT fails in [x, x+x^theta]. Computer-assisted optimization.
86	
87	**Relevance:** Tighter guarantees for binary search bracketing. Marginal practical impact since Visser's exponential bounds already give tight brackets.
88	
89	### 2.3 Connes: Letter Through Time (February 2026)
90	
91	**Source:** arXiv:2602.04022
92	
93	42-page survey plus novel "Letter to Riemann" approach:
94	- Using primes < 13, approximates first 50 zeta zeros to accuracies 2.6 x 10^{-55} to 10^{-3}
95	- Proves these approximating values lie EXACTLY on the critical line
96	- Connects Weil quadratic form to information theory
97	- Proposes proof strategy: convergence of zeros from finite to infinite Euler products
98	
99	**Relevance:** Fascinating analytic tool but NOT computational. No pathway to polylog prime counting. Our Session 9 analyzed the Connes-Weil quadratic form and found: "finds zeros but can't sum them fast."
100	
101	**Does it change the barrier?** No.
102	
103	### 2.4 Overall RH Status (April 2026)
104	
105	- 20 trillion zeros verified on critical line (Platt-Trudgian, 2021)
106	- De Bruijn-Newman constant Lambda = 0 (Rodgers-Tao, 2020)
107	- Lean 4 Mathlib: machine-checked proof of Prime Number Theorem
108	- RH remains OPEN after 167 years
109	
110	---
111	
112	## 3. Prime Distribution and Sieve Advances
113	
114	### 3.1 Lichtman: Modified Linear Sieve (Published 2025)
115	
116	**Source:** Algebra & Number Theory 19(1), 2025; arXiv:2109.02851
117	
118	Equidistributes primes in arithmetic progressions to moduli up to x^{10/17}, surpassing the x^{4/7} (BFI classical) and x^{7/12} (Maynard) barriers. New upper bound on twin prime count -- largest improvement since BFI (1986).
119	
120	**Relevance:** Fundamental sieve infrastructure. The x^{10/17} ~ x^{0.588} threshold is approaching the x^{2/3} ~ x^{0.667} used by Deleglise-Rivat but has not reached it.
121	
122	**Does it change the barrier?** No, but closing the gap between equidistribution threshold and algorithmic threshold.
123	
124	### 3.2 Pascadi: Exponents of Distribution to x^{5/8} (May 2025)
125	
126	**Source:** arXiv:2505.00653
127	
128	Primes and smooth numbers equidistributed in APs to moduli x^{5/8 - o(1)}. Eliminates dependency on Selberg eigenvalue conjecture. Uses triply-well-factorable weights and new large sieve inequalities for exceptional Maass forms.
129	
130	**Relevance:** x^{5/8} = x^{0.625} is tantalizingly close to x^{2/3} = x^{0.667}. If pushed to x^{2/3}, might enable algorithmic improvements to combinatorial pi(x). Currently below threshold.
131	
132	**Does it change the barrier?** Not yet, but worth monitoring.
133	
134	### 3.3 Le Duc Hieu: AP of Primes in Short Intervals (September 2025)
135	
136	**Source:** arXiv:2509.04883
137	
138	Once theta > 17/30, every sufficiently long interval [x, x+x^theta] contains many k-term APs of primes. Combines Guth-Maynard uniform short-interval PNT with Green-Tao transference principle.
139	
140	**Relevance:** Purely theoretical. Confirms richness of prime structure in computationally accessible intervals but provides no new algorithms.
141	
142	### 3.4 Visser: Exponential Bounds on Primes and Gaps (2025)
143	
144	**Sources:** Mathematics (MDPI) 13(11), 1844; Int. Math. Forum 2025; arXiv:2507.14410
145	
146	Three papers providing:
147	- Exponentially tight bounds on p(n) location
148	- Explicit bounds on prime gaps g(n) with proven validity ranges
149	- Analysis of Chebyshev theta at primes
150	
151	**Relevance:** Tighter bracketing for binary search when computing p(n). Reduces number of pi(x) evaluations needed.
152	
153	**Does it change the barrier?** No. Constant-factor improvement to search phase, not asymptotic.
154	
155	---
156	
157	## 4. Exact Formulas and Characterizations
158	
159	### 4.1 Ono-Craig-van Ittersum: Partition Detection of Primes (2024)
160	
161	**Source:** PNAS September 2024; arXiv:2405.06451
162	
163	n is prime iff certain equations in MacMahon partition functions M_k(n) hold. Infinitely many such characterizations exist.
164	
165	**Follow-up (2025):**
166	- Structural conjecture PROVED by two independent teams (Kane-Krishnamoorthy-Lau; van Ittersum-Mauth-Ono-Singh) using analytic and l-adic methods
167	- Extended to detect cubes of primes and primes in arithmetic progressions
168	- AMS Notices feature (June 2025); Cozzarelli Prize finalist
169	
170	**Relevance:** New theoretical lens. Computing M_k(n) is not competitive with sieving, but the extensions show the framework is more powerful than initially apparent.
171	
172	**Does it change the barrier?** No. Structural, not computational.
173	
174	### 4.2 Cloitre: Effective Analytic Recurrence (2025)
175	
176	**Source:** arXiv:2508.02690
177	
178	First proven effective analytic recurrence:
179	p(n+1) = ceil((-1 + zeta(2*p_n) * prod(1 - 1/p_j^{2*p_n}))^{-1/(2*p_n)})
180	
181	Exact for all n >= 1. No sieving -- pure zeta evaluation. Connects Gandhi's arithmetic approach with Golomb's analytic framework.
182	
183	**Relevance:** Theoretically significant as the first fully constructive analytic prime recurrence. Practically irrelevant: requires extreme-precision zeta evaluation.
184	
185	**Does it change the barrier?** No. Trades sieving for equally expensive zeta computation.
186	
187	### 4.3 Prunescu-Shunia: Arithmetic Terms (December 2024 - 2025)
188	
189	**Source:** arXiv:2412.14594; J. Integer Seq. 28, 2025
190	
191	First explicit fixed-length arithmetic terms for pi(n) and p(n). Uses only +, -, *, div, exp applied a fixed finite number of times.
192	
193	**The catch:** Tower-of-exponentials intermediate values. Even p(1)=2 requires computing integers with more digits than atoms in the observable universe. This is provably inherent (Mazzanti 2002): any arithmetic term encoding variable-length computation into fixed operations MUST use exponentially large intermediates.
194	
195	**Extended work (2025):** Arithmetic terms for Mersenne/Fermat/twin prime counting (arXiv:2512.01680); smallest/largest prime divisor (arXiv:2510.26939).
196	
197	**Relevance:** Settles Hardy-Wright's question: yes, a formula for p(n) exists. But it is "utterly useless for computation" as Hardy-Wright anticipated.
198	
199	**Does it change the barrier?** No. Theoretical existence result only.
200	
201	### 4.4 Saito: Mills' Constant Is Irrational (2024-2025)
202	
203	**Source:** arXiv:2404.19461; Mathematika 71(3), 2025
204	
205	Resolves long-standing open problem. Partial transcendence results obtained. The irrationality means Mills' constant cannot be expressed as a ratio of integers, but the formula floor(A^{3^n}) remains circular (computing A requires primes).
206	
207	**Does it change the barrier?** No.
208	
209	### 4.5 Bouras: New Prime-Generating Recurrence (2025)
210	
211	**Source:** arXiv:2509.09745
212	
213	GCD-based recurrence producing only 1s and primes, improving on Rowland (2008). Represented as finite continued fraction.
214	
215	**Relevance:** Does not produce ALL primes. Theoretical interest only.
216	
217	**Does it change the barrier?** No.
218	
219	---
220	
221	## 5. AI/ML in Mathematics
222	
223	### 5.1 AlphaEvolve (Google DeepMind, 2025-2026)
224	
225	Applied to 50+ open problems. In 20% of cases, improved on best known solutions:
226	- Improved lower bounds for 5 classical Ramsey numbers (records stood 10+ years)
227	- Erdos minimum overlap problem: improved upper bound
228	- New matrix multiplication methods
229	
230	**Assessment for primes:** Impressive for combinatorial optimization but NOT applicable to prime counting. The problem is exact computation, not optimization.
231	
232	### 5.2 AI Solving Erdos Problems (2025-2026)
233	
234	- ~100 Erdos problems moved to "solved" since October 2025 (per Tao's tracking)
235	- 15 solved since Christmas 2025; 11 credited AI involvement
236	- GPT-5.2 Pro solved Problem #397 (central binomial coefficients), verified by Tao
237	- Axiom AI solved 4 open problems including Chen-Gendron conjecture
238	- Ken Ono used AxiomProver for overnight proof derivation
239	
240	**Tao's caveat:** "AI is still nowhere near being able to solve major open problems." Most AI assistance is "souped-up literature search" + theorem assembly.
241	
242	**Does it change the barrier?** No. No AI has produced a new algorithm or formula for primes.
243	
244	### 5.3 Gauss Agent: Formalization (Math, Inc., 2026)
245	
246	Autoformalization agent for Lean proof verification. Formalized Strong Prime Number Theorem in 3 weeks (~25,000 lines of Lean, 1,000+ theorems). Previous human effort: 18+ months.
247	
248	**Assessment:** Important for verification infrastructure. Formalizing PNT does not yield faster prime algorithms.
249	
250	### 5.4 ML for Prime Classification
251	
252	The withdrawn paper (arXiv:2402.03363, 2024) was the most notable attempt -- ResNet + Transformer achieving ~99% recall, never 100%. Authors retracted it, stating the approach "does not lead to meaningful or valid conclusions."
253	
254	**Theoretical limitation:** Prime Coding Theorem (arXiv:2308.10817, 2023) proves information content of prime sequence exceeds any learnable pattern.
255	
256	**Does it change the barrier?** No. ML cannot achieve exactness for primes.
257	
258	---
259	
260	## 6. Practical Implementations
261	
262	### 6.1 Our Best Implementation
263	
264	**v7 Optimized Hybrid (Session 3):**
265	- R^{-1} + Lucy_Hedgehog DP + Newton/bisection + Miller-Rabin
266	- p(10^7) in 1.28s, p(10^8) in 7.73s
267	- 10,000/10,000 verified correct
268	
269	### 6.2 Theoretical Best for p(10^100)
270	
271	p(10^100) ~ 2.35 x 10^102. Computing it exactly requires:
272	- pi(x) at x ~ 10^102, costing O(x^{2/3}) = O(10^68) minimum operations
273	- Even analytic: O(x^{1/2+eps}) = O(10^{51+eps}) operations
274	- At 10^15 ops/sec: 10^36 seconds (billion billion billion years)
275	- Quantum: best O(x^{1/3}) = 10^34 quantum ops -> 10^25 seconds
276	
277	---
278	
279	## Updated Assessment (April 2026)
280	
281	### What has changed since the project began:
282	
283	1. **Aggarwal (2025)** provided rigorous proof that binary-search-on-pi(x) is provably superior to sieving for individual p(n). Validates our approach.
284	
285	2. **Guth-Maynard (2024)** broke an 80-year barrier in zero-density estimates. The most significant analytic number theory result in decades, but it improves distribution knowledge, not algorithms.
286	
287	3. **Prunescu-Shunia (2024-2025)** proved a formula for p(n) EXISTS as a fixed-length expression. But tower-exponential intermediates make it computationally useless.
288	
289	4. **Sieve equidistribution** (Lichtman x^{10/17}, Pascadi x^{5/8}) is creeping toward the x^{2/3} algorithmic threshold. Worth watching but no immediate impact.
290	
291	5. **AI tools** are accelerating mathematical research but have produced no new algorithms or complexity results for primes.
292	
293	6. **primecount** continues constant-factor improvements (SIMD, AVX512) but no asymptotic changes.
294	
295	### What has NOT changed:
296	
297	- **No new algorithm** for pi(x) or p(n) with better asymptotic complexity
298	- **No unconditional lower bound** beyond Omega(log x) for computing pi(x)
299	- **No polylogarithmic method** for pi(x) in any computational model
300	- **The O(x^{2/3}) combinatorial barrier** and **O(x^{1/2+eps}) analytic barrier** are intact
301	- **Quantum computing** still cannot break the O(x^{1/3}) barrier (Nayak-Wu lower bound)
302	- **The problem is genuinely OPEN** -- no proof exists that polylog is impossible
303	
304	### Bottom line:
305	
306	The problem of computing p(n) in polylogarithmic time remains open in the strongest theoretical sense (no impossibility proof), but all 380+ approaches across 10 sessions confirm the barrier empirically. The 2024-2026 literature -- including the most significant results in decades (Guth-Maynard, Prunescu-Shunia, Aggarwal) -- provides deeper understanding without changing the computational landscape. The gap between the trivial Omega(log x) lower bound and the O(x^{1/2+eps}) upper bound for pi(x) remains one of the least-explored frontiers in computational complexity.
307	
308	---
309

# State of the Art: Prime Computation (2024-2026)

Consolidated survey of all developments relevant to computing p(n) exactly.
Deduplicated from 6 overlapping source files.

Last updated: 2026-04-04

---

## 1. Prime Counting Algorithms

### 1.1 Combinatorial Methods (Best Practical)

**Current best asymptotic:** O(x^{2/3} / log^2 x) -- Deleglise-Rivat / Gourdon (1996/2001)

**Best implementation:** Kim Walisch's primecount library
- Version 8.4 released 2026-04-01
- Implements Gourdon's algorithm with SIMD acceleration (AVX512, ARM SVE)
- primecount 8.3 (March 2026): bidirectional clustered easy leaves, faster compression
- primecount 8.1 (January 2026): 15% faster AC.cpp
- primecount 8.0 (December 2025): new 128-bit API
- pi(10^25) computed in 330 CPU core hours on AMD EPYC Zen4
- The SIMD-accelerated Gourdon D formula in v8.4 vectorizes the hard special leaves bottleneck, estimated 2-4x speedup on modern CPUs

**Relevance to project:** This is the engine behind our best practical approach (binary search on pi(x)). Constant-factor improvements continue but no asymptotic breakthrough.

**Does it change the barrier?** No. Still O(x^{2/3}).

### 1.2 Analytic Methods (Best Theoretical)

**Current best asymptotic:** O(x^{1/2+eps}) -- Lagarias-Odlyzko (1987)

**Implementation status:**
- Platt's method: rigorous interval arithmetic, computed pi(10^25) unconditionally
- FKBJ method: Weil-Barner explicit formula with precomputed zeta zeros, 128-bit fixed-point
- In practice, 100x slower than combinatorial methods due to zeta zero computation overhead
- ~40,000 CPU core hours for pi(10^25) vs 330 for combinatorial

**Relevance to project:** Theoretically better asymptotics but impractical constants. The 100x gap persists for all currently reachable values of x.

**Does it change the barrier?** No. O(x^{1/2+eps}) has stood since 1987.

### 1.3 Aggarwal: Complexity of Computing p(n) (October 2025)

**Source:** arXiv:2510.16285

**Key results:**
- p(n) computable in O(sqrt(n) * (log n)^4) unconditionally via binary search on pi(x)
- Conditional (RH + Cramer): O(sqrt(n) * (log n)^{7/2} * log log n)
- Proves sieve-based methods CANNOT asymptotically beat binary-search-on-pi(x)
  - Best sieve for p(n): O(n log n / log log n)
  - Binary search + pi(x): O(sqrt(n) * polylog(n))
  - Binary search wins by factor ~sqrt(n)

**Relevance:** Most rigorous recent analysis. Confirms our implemented approach (Lucy_Hedgehog/Meissel-Lehmer + binary search) is provably superior to any sieve for computing a single p(n).

**Does it change the barrier?** No new algorithm, but provides the tightest known complexity analysis.

---

## 2. Riemann Hypothesis and Zero-Density Estimates

### 2.1 Guth-Maynard: Large Value Estimates (May 2024)

**Source:** arXiv:2405.20552; published in Annals of Mathematics, 2025

**The breakthrough:**
- First improvement to Ingham's 1940 zero-density bound in 80+ years
- Improved exponent from 3/5 = 0.6 to 13/25 = 0.52
- New zero-density estimate: N(sigma, T) <= T^{30(1-sigma)/13 + o(1)}

**Propagated results:**
- PNT in short intervals: [x, x + x^{17/30}], improving from x^{3/5}
- Almost all short intervals: theta improved from 1/6 to 2/15
- Tao: "a remarkable achievement"; Radziwill: "first new idea in the hunt for zeta zeros in 50 years"

**Relevance:** Does NOT directly yield a new algorithm, but tightens theoretical guarantees about prime distribution. Better zero-density bounds could reduce the number of zeta zeros needed for rigorous analytic pi(x) computation; practical impact unclear.

**Does it change the barrier?** No. Improves density estimates, not computational methods.

### 2.2 Gafni-Tao: Exceptional Intervals (May 2025)

**Source:** arXiv:2505.24017

Makes Guth-Maynard results explicit and quantitative for short intervals. Provides explicit upper bounds on exceptional set where PNT fails in [x, x+x^theta]. Computer-assisted optimization.

**Relevance:** Tighter guarantees for binary search bracketing. Marginal practical impact since Visser's exponential bounds already give tight brackets.

### 2.3 Connes: Letter Through Time (February 2026)

**Source:** arXiv:2602.04022

42-page survey plus novel "Letter to Riemann" approach:
- Using primes < 13, approximates first 50 zeta zeros to accuracies 2.6 x 10^{-55} to 10^{-3}
- Proves these approximating values lie EXACTLY on the critical line
- Connects Weil quadratic form to information theory
- Proposes proof strategy: convergence of zeros from finite to infinite Euler products

**Relevance:** Fascinating analytic tool but NOT computational. No pathway to polylog prime counting. Our Session 9 analyzed the Connes-Weil quadratic form and found: "finds zeros but can't sum them fast."

**Does it change the barrier?** No.

### 2.4 Overall RH Status (April 2026)

- 20 trillion zeros verified on critical line (Platt-Trudgian, 2021)
- De Bruijn-Newman constant Lambda = 0 (Rodgers-Tao, 2020)
- Lean 4 Mathlib: machine-checked proof of Prime Number Theorem
- RH remains OPEN after 167 years

---

## 3. Prime Distribution and Sieve Advances

### 3.1 Lichtman: Modified Linear Sieve (Published 2025)

**Source:** Algebra & Number Theory 19(1), 2025; arXiv:2109.02851

Equidistributes primes in arithmetic progressions to moduli up to x^{10/17}, surpassing the x^{4/7} (BFI classical) and x^{7/12} (Maynard) barriers. New upper bound on twin prime count -- largest improvement since BFI (1986).

**Relevance:** Fundamental sieve infrastructure. The x^{10/17} ~ x^{0.588} threshold is approaching the x^{2/3} ~ x^{0.667} used by Deleglise-Rivat but has not reached it.

**Does it change the barrier?** No, but closing the gap between equidistribution threshold and algorithmic threshold.

**Relevance:** x^{5/8} = x^{0.625} is tantalizingly close to x^{2/3} = x^{0.667}. If pushed to x^{2/3}, might enable algorithmic improvements to combinatorial pi(x). Currently below threshold.

**Does it change the barrier?** Not yet, but worth monitoring.

### 3.3 Le Duc Hieu: AP of Primes in Short Intervals (September 2025)

**Source:** arXiv:2509.04883

Once theta > 17/30, every sufficiently long interval [x, x+x^theta] contains many k-term APs of primes. Combines Guth-Maynard uniform short-interval PNT with Green-Tao transference principle.

**Relevance:** Purely theoretical. Confirms richness of prime structure in computationally accessible intervals but provides no new algorithms.

### 3.4 Visser: Exponential Bounds on Primes and Gaps (2025)

**Sources:** Mathematics (MDPI) 13(11), 1844; Int. Math. Forum 2025; arXiv:2507.14410

Three papers providing:
- Exponentially tight bounds on p(n) location
- Explicit bounds on prime gaps g(n) with proven validity ranges
- Analysis of Chebyshev theta at primes

**Relevance:** Tighter bracketing for binary search when computing p(n). Reduces number of pi(x) evaluations needed.

**Does it change the barrier?** No. Constant-factor improvement to search phase, not asymptotic.

---

## 4. Exact Formulas and Characterizations

### 4.1 Ono-Craig-van Ittersum: Partition Detection of Primes (2024)

**Source:** PNAS September 2024; arXiv:2405.06451

n is prime iff certain equations in MacMahon partition functions M_k(n) hold. Infinitely many such characterizations exist.

**Follow-up (2025):**
- Structural conjecture PROVED by two independent teams (Kane-Krishnamoorthy-Lau; van Ittersum-Mauth-Ono-Singh) using analytic and l-adic methods
- Extended to detect cubes of primes and primes in arithmetic progressions
- AMS Notices feature (June 2025); Cozzarelli Prize finalist

**Relevance:** New theoretical lens. Computing M_k(n) is not competitive with sieving, but the extensions show the framework is more powerful than initially apparent.

**Does it change the barrier?** No. Structural, not computational.

### 4.2 Cloitre: Effective Analytic Recurrence (2025)

**Source:** arXiv:2508.02690

First proven effective analytic recurrence:
p(n+1) = ceil((-1 + zeta(2*p_n) * prod(1 - 1/p_j^{2*p_n}))^{-1/(2*p_n)})

Exact for all n >= 1. No sieving -- pure zeta evaluation. Connects Gandhi's arithmetic approach with Golomb's analytic framework.

**Relevance:** Theoretically significant as the first fully constructive analytic prime recurrence. Practically irrelevant: requires extreme-precision zeta evaluation.

**Does it change the barrier?** No. Trades sieving for equally expensive zeta computation.

### 4.3 Prunescu-Shunia: Arithmetic Terms (December 2024 - 2025)

**Source:** arXiv:2412.14594; J. Integer Seq. 28, 2025

First explicit fixed-length arithmetic terms for pi(n) and p(n). Uses only +, -, *, div, exp applied a fixed finite number of times.

**The catch:** Tower-of-exponentials intermediate values. Even p(1)=2 requires computing integers with more digits than atoms in the observable universe. This is provably inherent (Mazzanti 2002): any arithmetic term encoding variable-length computation into fixed operations MUST use exponentially large intermediates.

**Extended work (2025):** Arithmetic terms for Mersenne/Fermat/twin prime counting (arXiv:2512.01680); smallest/largest prime divisor (arXiv:2510.26939).

**Relevance:** Settles Hardy-Wright's question: yes, a formula for p(n) exists. But it is "utterly useless for computation" as Hardy-Wright anticipated.

### 4.4 Saito: Mills' Constant Is Irrational (2024-2025)

**Source:** arXiv:2404.19461; Mathematika 71(3), 2025

Resolves long-standing open problem. Partial transcendence results obtained. The irrationality means Mills' constant cannot be expressed as a ratio of integers, but the formula floor(A^{3^n}) remains circular (computing A requires primes).

**Does it change the barrier?** No.

### 4.5 Bouras: New Prime-Generating Recurrence (2025)

**Source:** arXiv:2509.09745

GCD-based recurrence producing only 1s and primes, improving on Rowland (2008). Represented as finite continued fraction.

**Relevance:** Does not produce ALL primes. Theoretical interest only.

**Does it change the barrier?** No.

---

## 5. AI/ML in Mathematics

### 5.1 AlphaEvolve (Google DeepMind, 2025-2026)

Applied to 50+ open problems. In 20% of cases, improved on best known solutions:
- Improved lower bounds for 5 classical Ramsey numbers (records stood 10+ years)
- Erdos minimum overlap problem: improved upper bound
- New matrix multiplication methods

**Assessment for primes:** Impressive for combinatorial optimization but NOT applicable to prime counting. The problem is exact computation, not optimization.

### 5.2 AI Solving Erdos Problems (2025-2026)

- ~100 Erdos problems moved to "solved" since October 2025 (per Tao's tracking)
- 15 solved since Christmas 2025; 11 credited AI involvement
- GPT-5.2 Pro solved Problem #397 (central binomial coefficients), verified by Tao
- Axiom AI solved 4 open problems including Chen-Gendron conjecture
- Ken Ono used AxiomProver for overnight proof derivation

**Tao's caveat:** "AI is still nowhere near being able to solve major open problems." Most AI assistance is "souped-up literature search" + theorem assembly.

### 5.3 Gauss Agent: Formalization (Math, Inc., 2026)

### 5.4 ML for Prime Classification

The withdrawn paper (arXiv:2402.03363, 2024) was the most notable attempt -- ResNet + Transformer achieving ~99% recall, never 100%. Authors retracted it, stating the approach "does not lead to meaningful or valid conclusions."

**Theoretical limitation:** Prime Coding Theorem (arXiv:2308.10817, 2023) proves information content of prime sequence exceeds any learnable pattern.

**Does it change the barrier?** No. ML cannot achieve exactness for primes.

---

## 6. Practical Implementations

### 6.1 Our Best Implementation

**v7 Optimized Hybrid (Session 3):**
- R^{-1} + Lucy_Hedgehog DP + Newton/bisection + Miller-Rabin
- p(10^7) in 1.28s, p(10^8) in 7.73s
- 10,000/10,000 verified correct

### 6.2 Theoretical Best for p(10^100)

p(10^100) ~ 2.35 x 10^102. Computing it exactly requires:

- At 10^15 ops/sec: 10^36 seconds (billion billion billion years)
- Quantum: best O(x^{1/3}) = 10^34 quantum ops -> 10^25 seconds

---

## Updated Assessment (April 2026)

### What has changed since the project began:

1. **Aggarwal (2025)** provided rigorous proof that binary-search-on-pi(x) is provably superior to sieving for individual p(n). Validates our approach.

2. **Guth-Maynard (2024)** broke an 80-year barrier in zero-density estimates. The most significant analytic number theory result in decades, but it improves distribution knowledge, not algorithms.

3. **Prunescu-Shunia (2024-2025)** proved a formula for p(n) EXISTS as a fixed-length expression. But tower-exponential intermediates make it computationally useless.

4. **Sieve equidistribution** (Lichtman x^{10/17}, Pascadi x^{5/8}) is creeping toward the x^{2/3} algorithmic threshold. Worth watching but no immediate impact.

5. **AI tools** are accelerating mathematical research but have produced no new algorithms or complexity results for primes.

6. **primecount** continues constant-factor improvements (SIMD, AVX512) but no asymptotic changes.

### What has NOT changed:

- **No new algorithm** for pi(x) or p(n) with better asymptotic complexity
- **No unconditional lower bound** beyond Omega(log x) for computing pi(x)
- **No polylogarithmic method** for pi(x) in any computational model
- **The O(x^{2/3}) combinatorial barrier** and **O(x^{1/2+eps}) analytic barrier** are intact
- **Quantum computing** still cannot break the O(x^{1/3}) barrier (Nayak-Wu lower bound)

### Bottom line:

The problem of computing p(n) in polylogarithmic time remains open in the strongest theoretical sense (no impossibility proof), but all 380+ approaches across 10 sessions confirm the barrier empirically. The 2024-2026 literature -- including the most significant results in decades (Guth-Maynard, Prunescu-Shunia, Aggarwal) -- provides deeper understanding without changing the computational landscape. The gap between the trivial Omega(log x) lower bound and the O(x^{1/2+eps}) upper bound for pi(x) remains one of the least-explored frontiers in computational complexity.

input.prompt:
RESEARCH TASK (no code writing needed — just deep mathematical analysis):

We are trying to compute pi(x) (the prime counting function) in O(polylog(x)) time. All 400+ approaches tested have failed. The core barrier: all known methods compute O(sqrt(x)) intermediate values, giving exponential-size circuits in N = log(x).

I want you to deeply analyze the ALGEBRAIC GEOMETRY / POINT COUNTING angle:

**Key observation**: Schoof's algorithm computes #E(F_p) = p + 1 - a_p for an elliptic curve E over F_p in polynomial time in log(p). The Hasse-Weil L-function L(E, s) encodes these a_p values. And the Sato-Tate distribution governs the statistics of a_p/2√p.

**Question 1**: Can we construct a family of varieties V_x such that #V_x(F_q) encodes pi(x) for appropriate q? That is, can we reduce counting primes to counting points on varieties?

**Question 2**: If we pick a specific elliptic curve E and compute a_p for many primes p, we get the L-function L(E,s). The Birch-Swinnerton-Dyer conjecture relates L(E,1) to arithmetic invariants. Is there any known or possible connection between L-function SPECIAL VALUES and the prime counting function?

**Question 3**: Over function fields F_q[T], the analog of pi(x) counts irreducible polynomials of degree ≤ d, which has an EXACT formula: sum_{k|d} μ(d/k) q^k / d. This is O(polylog) to compute! What specifically breaks when we try to transfer this to Z? (We know "genus = infinity" was the answer in Session 7, but can we make this more precise?)

**Question 4**: Can the WEIL CONJECTURES framework help? The zeta function of a variety over F_q has a rationality structure that constrains point counts. Is there an analog where the Riemann zeta function's zeros constrain pi(x) in a way that could be exploited computationally?

Think carefully about each. Reference relevant theorems and papers. Explain WHY each direction succeeds or fails. Look for any non-obvious connection.

Write up your analysis as a structured report. Check the project's closed paths file at /apps/aplikacijos/prime-research/status/CLOSED_PATHS.md to avoid rediscovering things already tried.

input.prompt:
RESEARCH + EXPERIMENT TASK:

Context: We want to compute pi(x) = #{primes ≤ x} in O(polylog(x)) time. All 400+ approaches have failed. The barrier: all methods compute O(sqrt(x)) intermediate values.

**The core mathematical question**: Can pi(x) be expressed as a sum/product of O(poly(log x)) terms, each efficiently computable?

I want you to investigate the following specific angles and run experiments:

### A) Generating function evaluation at specific points

The prime zeta function P(s) = sum_{p prime} p^{-s} satisfies:
ln(zeta(s)) = sum_{k=1}^{infty} P(ks)/k

So P(s) = ln(zeta(s)) - P(2s)/2 - P(3s)/3 - ...

Can we extract pi(x) from evaluating P(s) at specific s values without computing individual primes?

Write a Python experiment: for small x (up to 10000), compute pi(x) by:
1. Evaluating zeta(s) at s = 2, 3, 4, ... (known values)
2. Using Möbius inversion: P(s) = sum_{k=1}^{infty} mu(k)/k * ln(zeta(ks))
3. Then try to extract pi(x) from P(s) values via Perron's formula or similar

Does this give a DIFFERENT computational path than the standard explicit formula?

### B) Polynomial identity for pi(x) mod small numbers

For each prime q, the Legendre symbol gives a way to test if n is a quadratic residue mod q. The number of primes in [1,x] that are ≡ a (mod q) is approximately pi(x)/(q-1) with correction terms from L-functions.

Experiment: for x up to 100000, compute:
- pi(x) mod 2, mod 3, mod 5, mod 7 separately
- The correction terms for pi(x; q, a) = pi(x)/(q-1) + error
- Can we reconstruct pi(x) from pi(x) mod {2,3,5,...,P} using CRT with fewer than sqrt(x) operations?

This was partially explored (pi(x) mod m has invariant entropy 0.537), but the CRT RECONSTRUCTION angle may not have been fully tested.

### C) Recursive identity search

Search for any identity of the form:
pi(x) = f(pi(x/2), pi(x/3), pi(x/5), ..., pi(x/p_k)) + correction

where the correction is efficiently computable and the recursion depth is O(log x).

Test empirically for x up to 100000: what is the minimal set of subproblem evaluations needed to exactly recover pi(x)?

Save all experiments to /apps/aplikacijos/prime-research/experiments/other/ with descriptive filenames. Report results clearly.

input.prompt:
RESEARCH + EXPERIMENT TASK:

Context: We want to show PRIMES is in TC^0 (constant-depth, polynomial-size threshold circuits). Session 12 showed:
- GRH → PRIMES in TC^0 (via Miller's deterministic test: only uses modular exponentiation which IS in TC^0)
- BPSW correctness → PRIMES in TC^0 (uses scalar + matrix power mod 2, both TC^0)
- AKS path BLOCKED: requires growing-dimension matrix powering, which is at TC^0/NC^1 boundary

The KEY remaining question: Is there a NON-AKS TC^0 primality test that works unconditionally?

Investigate these specific angles:

### 1. Wilson's theorem approach
(n-1)! ≡ -1 (mod n) iff n is prime. Computing n! mod n requires computing (n-1)! mod n.
- Is factorial mod n computable in TC^0?
- Shamir (1991) showed factorial IS in GapL ⊂ NC^2
- What about mod a SPECIFIC n? For fixed n, (n-1)! mod n is a constant...
- The issue is computing this for variable n. Is modular factorial in TC^0?

### 2. Lucas sequence approach  
For prime p and appropriate P: U_{p}(P, 1) ≡ 0 (mod p).
Lucas sequences satisfy a LINEAR recurrence, which is matrix powering of a 2x2 matrix.
2x2 matrix powering IS in TC^0 (fixed dimension ≤ 2).
So: U_n(P, Q) mod n is in TC^0.
Can we build a primality test from ONLY 2x2 matrix powering (Lucas sequences)?
- BPSW uses Lucas, but is it SUFFICIENT without the Fermat test?
- Strong Lucas probable prime test: how many composites survive?

### 3. Quadratic Frobenius test
Grantham (1998): QFT uses Frobenius automorphism in Z[x]/(x^2 - bx - c, n).
This involves 2x2 matrix powering (Frobenius map on the extension).
Is QFT in TC^0? Is it deterministic for some parameter choice?

### 4. Sum-of-two-squares characterization
Fermat: p ≡ 1 (mod 4) is prime iff p = a^2 + b^2 uniquely.
Computing whether n is a sum of two squares... uses Cornacchia's algorithm (GCD-like, in NC).
Can this be adapted to a TC^0 primality test for primes ≡ 1 (mod 4)?

For each approach:
- Analyze theoretically whether it can work in TC^0
- If promising, write a Python experiment testing it numerically
- Check /apps/aplikacijos/prime-research/status/CLOSED_PATHS.md for prior attempts

Save experiments to /apps/aplikacijos/prime-research/experiments/circuit_complexity/
Report the theoretical analysis AND experimental results.

input.prompt:
RESEARCH + EXPERIMENT TASK:

Context: We want to compute pi(x) in O(polylog(x)) time. All 400+ approaches have failed. I'm exploring a possibly new angle based on spectral graph theory.

**Idea**: Consider the Cayley graph G of Z/NZ with generating set S = primes up to N. Or alternatively, consider the "coprimality graph" where vertices are {1,...,x} and edges connect coprime pairs.

### Angle 1: Ramanujan graphs and primes

Lubotzky-Phillips-Sarnak (1988) constructed Ramanujan graphs using primes. The spectral gap of these graphs is optimal. The NUMBER of vertices/edges encodes information about primes.

Question: Can the spectral gap or other spectral invariants of a Cayley graph constructed WITHOUT knowing individual primes somehow encode pi(x)?

For example, consider G_x = Cayley(Z/xZ, {1,...,x} \ composites). The eigenvalues of G_x's adjacency matrix relate to character sums. Can we compute eigenvalue-related quantities without knowing the generators?

### Angle 2: Ihara zeta function of graphs

The Ihara zeta function of a graph G is:
Z_G(u) = prod_{[C]} (1 - u^{|C|})^{-1}

where [C] ranges over equivalence classes of prime cycles. For Cayley graphs, this connects to the Riemann zeta function.

If we construct a GRAPH whose Ihara zeta function factors in a way that reveals pi(x)...

### Angle 3: Expander mixing lemma

For a d-regular Ramanujan graph on n vertices with second eigenvalue λ:
|e(S,T) - d|S||T|/n| ≤ λ sqrt(|S||T|)

If primes form one of S or T, can this give us a count with error < 1?

### Experiment

Write Python code to:
1. For x = 100, 1000, 10000: construct the Cayley graph of Z/xZ with generators = primes up to x
2. Compute the eigenvalues of the adjacency matrix (or a related matrix)
3. Check if any spectral quantity correlates exactly with pi(x)
4. Test whether the number of closed walks of length k in this graph encodes prime information

Save experiments to /apps/aplikacijos/prime-research/experiments/other/spectral_graph_primes.py

Check /apps/aplikacijos/prime-research/status/CLOSED_PATHS.md for prior attempts (graph spectrum approach, expander graphs were tried but this SPECIFIC Cayley graph / Ihara angle may be new).

Report theoretical analysis and numerical results.

content.thinking:
Good, I have 5 agents running in background. Let me work on a different angle myself while they run — I'll investigate the most promising theoretical direction.

Let me think about what I can do in the main thread that doesn't overlap with the agents:

1. Literature search — agent doing this
2. Algebraic geometry — agent doing this
3. Compressed counting — agent doing this
4. TC^0 primality — agent doing this
5. Spectral graph — agent doing this

I should work on something different. Let me think about the DEEPEST theoretical question:

**Can we express pi(x) using a fundamentally different mathematical framework?**
 
The breakthrough from Session 12 was recognizing that "Is pi(x) in NC?" reduces to whether we can compute it with polylogarithmic depth — but every approach so far requires exponential circuits because we're forced to compute O(sqrt(x)) intermediate values. What if I bypass that entirely and look for a direct formula instead? In the function field analogy over F_q[T], there's an elegant closed form for counting irreducible polynomials of degree d using Möbius inversion, and the prime counting analog follows a similar pattern.

The crucial observation is that this function field version computes in O(d^2) = O((log x)^2) time — genuinely polylogarithmic. The reason it works is structural: the zeta function for F_q[T] is 1/(1-qu), which has no zeros, so there are no oscillatory corrections to worry about. The smooth part of the distribution IS the answer. But for the integers, the Riemann zeta function has infinitely many zeros, and those oscillations are the entire obstruction to a direct formula. The question becomes whether there's a deformation or interpolation from the function field case back to the number field case — which points toward F_1 geometry as a potential framework.

Now I'm thinking about how the zeta function behaves differently across these settings. For F_q[T], the formula ζ_{F_q[T]}(s) = 1/(1-q^{1-s}) makes the Riemann hypothesis trivially true since there are no zeros at all. But for curves of genus g over F_q, the zeta function picks up 2g zeros from the Weil conjectures, and the point-counting formula involves summing over these algebraic integers.

The key insight is that if I could somehow reduce the prime-counting problem to counting points on a curve with bounded genus, the computation would be tractable in O(g * polylog) time. The problem is that Spec(Z) itself has infinite effective genus—the Riemann zeta zeros essentially encode this unbounded complexity.

So maybe instead of counting all primes up to x directly, I should try decomposing π(x) into contributions from different pieces of the spectrum, where each piece has manageable complexity. The zeros up to height T contribute O(T) oscillatory terms, and to pin down π(x) exactly I'd need roughly sqrt(x) zeros. The GUE statistics suggest each zero carries at most O(1) bits of information toward the final answer.

But here's where it gets strange: π(x) itself is only N bits, yet the oscillatory sum involves roughly 2^{N/2} terms that somehow cancel down to an O(N)-bit result. This is like summing 2^{N/2} random ±1 values that miraculously produce a small answer—normally the central limit theorem would give O(2^{N/4}), but the actual error bound is tighter, around O(2^{N/2} * log(x)).

Let me shift gears entirely and explore whether representation theory of the symmetric group offers insight. There's a deep connection between prime factorization patterns and permutation cycle structures—Billingsley's theorem shows that the distribution of prime factors in a random integer mirrors the cycle lengths of a random permutation. Maybe the prime counting function relates to counting permutations with specific structural properties.

Now I'm wondering if circuit complexity barriers might apply here. Razborov's monotone circuit lower bounds for clique suggest similar obstacles could exist for computing π(x), but primality itself isn't a monotone function—flipping a single bit can change whether a number is prime or composite. So Boolean circuit approaches seem blocked. Let me consider instead what happens with arithmetic circuits over the integers.

An arithmetic circuit using addition, subtraction, and multiplication could theoretically compute π(x), but the divisibility checks and floor operations needed to identify primes aren't naturally expressible as arithmetic operations. The Möbius function approach also runs into the same wall. This suggests the arithmetic circuit model itself might be the wrong framework for this problem. Maybe I should think about whether we can compute π(x) modulo various numbers and then reconstruct the answer.

Computing π(x) modulo m turns out to be just as hard as computing π(x) itself, regardless of which m we choose. Even if I use the Chinese Remainder Theorem and compute π(x) modulo each prime up to roughly x/ln(x), I'd need information about an enormous number of primes. The entropy of π(x) mod p appears to be invariant across different primes, so there's no hidden structure to exploit there either.

Let me shift focus to a different approach: what if I approximate π(x) using the Riemann R function, which gives a much tighter estimate than the prime number theorem, and then compute just the correction term needed to get the exact value?

The error between π(x) and R(x) is relatively small—on the order of √x · log(x)—so if I could compute this correction modulo small numbers and use the fact that it has limited bit length, maybe I could reconstruct π(x) without computing it directly. But this still requires knowing π(x) modulo various m, which brings me back to the same hard problem.

I'm going in circles with these approaches. Maybe the real question isn't how to compute π(x) efficiently, but whether it's actually impossible to do so in polylogarithmic time. If I could prove that π(x) lies outside NC—the class of problems solvable in polylogarithmic time with polynomial processors—that would be a major breakthrough in itself.

The challenge is that proving such a lower bound would require separating NC from P in the binary input model, which is far beyond what current complexity theory can handle. We can't even prove that explicit functions lie outside NC beyond what we already know from AC⁰ lower bounds. So a rigorous lower bound proof seems out of reach with existing techniques, which suggests the problem will likely remain open unless there's some positive result I'm missing.

Now I'm wondering if there's actually a constructive approach hiding here. A polynomial-size circuit for computing π(x) would need to somehow evaluate the oscillatory correction term involving the Riemann zeros without explicitly enumerating them. This would require the zeros themselves to have some exploitable structure—perhaps a compressed representation that allows the sum over zero heights to be computed efficiently rather than term-by-term.

The key insight is that each zero contributes a phase term e^{i·γₙ·ln(x)}, and when I sum these contributions across all zeros up to height T ~ √x, I'm essentially computing a trigonometric sum with irrational frequencies. The question becomes whether such sums can be evaluated in polylogarithmic time, which seems to be the fundamental barrier.

Fast multipole methods were already explored but failed because the zero heights are incommensurable — there's no underlying grid structure to exploit. The spectral analysis shows that 91% of the variance behaves like random noise (GUE statistics), but that remaining 9% of structure comes from the global distribution of zeros and their low-order correlations. Even if I could extract that 9%, it would only give an approximate answer, not the exact value I need.

Let me shift perspective and think about this through information theory — the key observation is that π(x) contains O(N) bits of information, so computing it exactly requires accessing that much information somehow.

The real question becomes whether there's a way to compute these O(N) output bits using only polynomially many intermediate values, rather than the exponential number that current methods require. The prime indicators have multiplicative structure — composites are determined by their smallest prime factor, and divisibility relationships create dependencies — which is exactly what the classical sieve-based methods like Lehmer's algorithm exploit to reduce the computation.

But those approaches still bottleneck at needing O(2^{N/2}) floor values. I'm wondering if there's a fundamentally different way to leverage that multiplicative structure without going through floor computations at all. Maybe there's something in additive structure — like how Goldbach and Vinogradov results show every large number is a sum of a few primes — though those give existence rather than counts.

Let me try something concrete that might be novel: applying number field sieve techniques to prime counting directly. The NFS factors integers efficiently by working simultaneously in two factor bases and finding relations between them. What if I adapt that framework to counting primes instead?

Actually, I realize that's essentially what Meissel-Lehmer already does — it breaks the count into contributions from primes of different sizes. The number field trick doesn't transfer because we're counting, not factoring.

Let me explore a different direction: what if I could simulate having a prime oracle without actually testing primality for each number? I could express whether n is prime as a formula involving divisibility checks, then try to count those efficiently.

The key insight is that for each divisor d, I can compute how many multiples of d exist in the range [2,x] using floor(x/d) - 1, rather than checking divisibility for each individual n.

But when I try to sum this over all divisors, I run into the classic inclusion-exclusion problem—composites with multiple small prime factors get counted multiple times. This is just Legendre's sieve formula in disguise, which I keep circling back to.

Let me try a completely different angle: the arithmetic derivative. Instead of factoring directly, I can use the derivative property where primes have derivative 1, and the Leibniz rule applies to products. This might give me a way to identify primes without explicit factorization.

The key insight is that n' = 1 exactly when n is prime, since n' = n times the sum of reciprocals of its prime divisors. But computing the derivative for each number still requires knowing the prime factors, so I'm back to the same computational wall.

I'm exploring whether additive energy and Fourier analysis on the primes could bypass factorization, but the estimates only give asymptotic bounds on pi(x), not exact values. Rather than continue chasing theoretical dead ends, I should focus on something concrete—testing whether the Selberg integral offers a computational path to pi(x) that doesn't rely on traditional sieving.

The Selberg sieve itself only provides bounds through optimized weights, not exact counts. Legendre's formula gives the exact answer via inclusion-exclusion but requires exponentially many terms. I'm wondering if a weighted variant of the sieve could converge to the true count more efficiently, though the parity barrier in sieve theory suggests there are fundamental limits to how tight these bounds can get.

The parity barrier is the real obstacle—sieves of dimension 1 can't distinguish between numbers with an even versus odd number of prime factors, so they're inherently limited to giving bounds. Friedlander-Iwaniec and Heath-Brown managed to break this barrier for special forms like a² + b⁴ and a³ + 2b³ by exploiting their algebraic structure, but general primes lack such structure. I'm now thinking about whether Maynard's approach might offer a different angle here.

Actually, I should reconsider this through the lens of what came up in Session 12—the DFT obstruction. The key finding was that commutative ring structure reduces to DFT operations with a depth of O(log log n), and this DFT depth is the precise residual obstruction at the TC⁰/NC¹ boundary. Let me work through what this means for the AKS primality test: it uses the ring Z_n[x]/(x^r - 1) where r is a suitable prime order, and this ring decomposes via DFT into a product of cyclotomic fields.

The test checks whether (x + a)^n ≡ x^n + a holds in this ring, which reduces to verifying the condition at each r-th root of unity—essentially checking that (ω^j + a)^n ≡ ω^{jn} + a for all j. Each individual check is a scalar modular exponentiation in TC⁰, and with r = polylog(n), we have polylog parallel checks. The DFT itself is what ties this together: we're evaluating the polynomial (x+a)^n - (x^n + a) at all r-th roots of unity, which is fundamentally a DFT computation on the coefficient vector.

Now I'm wondering whether computing the DFT of a polylog-length vector can be done in constant depth. The DFT is really just a matrix-vector product with the Vandermonde matrix of roots of unity, and for r = O(polylog(n)), this is a polylog-dimensional matrix. Since matrix-vector multiplication with polylog dimensions should be in TC⁰—it's just inner products, which are counting operations—the question becomes whether the DFT matrix-vector product itself stays in TC⁰. Each inner product involves multiplying pairs of O(N)-bit numbers and summing them, which is the core operation to analyze.

But wait, I'm second-guessing myself because Session 12 claimed that O(log log n) depth is the precise obstruction. Let me reconsider what's actually being computed—the DFT isn't of a fixed vector, but of coefficients from (x+a)^n mod (x^r - 1, n), which means the input itself depends on the problem structure in a way I need to think through more carefully. (x+a)^n mod (x^r-1, n) is the matrix powering step.

Let me think again. In the decomposition:
Z_n[x]/(x^r - 1) ≅ Z_n[x]/(Φ_1) × Z_n[x]/(Φ_2) × ... 

via CRT. Each factor is Z_n[ω] for a primitive d-th root of unity.

The AKS test: check (a + x)^n = a + x^n in this ring. After DFT decomposition into the factors, this becomes: for each factor Z_n[ω], check (a + ω)^n = a + ω^n.

Each such check IS a scalar modular exponentiation, which IS in TC^0.

The DFT step just routes the computation — it maps the polynomial representation to evaluations at roots of unity. But I don't need to compute (x+a)^n and then apply DFT; I can directly compute (a + ω^j)^n for each j in parallel, which is scalar modular exponentiation. So the AKS test becomes: pick r and a, then for each j from 0 to r-1, compute (a + ω^j)^n mod n and verify it equals (a + ω^{jn mod r}), with all r checks running in parallel in TC^0. This requires precomputed r-th roots of unity mod n and scalar exponentiations.

But here's the issue: ω is a primitive r-th root of unity modulo n. For prime n, this exists only if r divides n-1. For composite n, it might not exist in Z_n, or might only exist in Z_n[x]/(Φ_r(x)). In AKS, r is chosen as the smallest integer where ord_r(n) > (log n)^2, and we work in Z_n[x]/(x^r - 1). This ring doesn't necessarily contain r-th roots of unity when n is composite, so the CRT decomposition doesn't cleanly apply.

The real problem is that for composite n, the ring Z_n[x]/(x^r - 1) has zero divisors and the DFT becomes ill-defined. The AKS test is designed to detect primality—it works perfectly when n is prime, but fails for composites. For a TC^0 circuit that must handle all inputs, we can't rely on the DFT working uniformly. So instead of using DFT, we'd need to directly compute the polynomial operations in the ring without assuming the roots of unity exist.

Computing (x + a)^n mod (x^r - 1, n) means raising a degree-(r-1) polynomial to the n-th power using cyclic convolution for multiplication. With repeated squaring, this takes O(r^2 * log n) modular multiplications, and since r is polylogarithmic in n, the total cost is polylogarithmic. Each modular multiplication itself is computable in TC^0, so the whole operation fits within the complexity class.

But the sequential dependency chain of O(log n) squaring steps creates a depth problem—each step must wait for the previous one. However, for scalar modular exponentiation, there's a way around repeated squaring: instead of building up the result iteratively, I can compute all bits of a^n mod m simultaneously in parallel, which brings the whole thing into TC^0.

The approach uses the binary representation of n to express a^n as a product of precomputed powers a^{2^k} mod m, then multiplies all these terms together. Since both iterated multiplication of many numbers and modular reduction via division are known to be in TC^0 (from Hesse-Allender-Barrington), the entire computation stays within TC^0 depth.

For polynomial exponentiation, though, the situation gets more complex—I'm now dealing with iterated multiplication of polynomials rather than integers, where each polynomial has r coefficients of O(M) bits, and multiplying two degree-r polynomials produces a degree-2r result. Computing the product of N such polynomials across all the exponentiation steps is essentially equivalent to matrix multiplication, which introduces additional structural constraints.

When working modulo (x^r - 1, m), polynomial multiplication becomes a ring operation that can be represented as r × r matrix powering—specifically, computing M^n where M encodes the polynomial f. This is where the real bottleneck emerges: for r = O(polylog(n)), the matrix dimension grows with the input, placing this problem right at the TC^0/NC^1 boundary that I identified earlier. If r were constant, this would cleanly fall into TC^0, but the growing dimension is the obstacle. There's an additional structure I'm noticing here—these matrices are circulant, which might offer some leverage.

Circulant matrices have a special property: they're diagonalized by the DFT matrix. If I can apply the DFT, then powering the circulant matrix reduces to r independent scalar exponentiations, which are definitely in TC^0. So the real question becomes whether computing the DFT of a polylog-length vector over Z_n stays within TC^0. For a prime n where r divides (n-1), a primitive r-th root of unity exists in Z_n, and each DFT coefficient is just a sum of r products—all computable in TC^0. When n is composite, the root of unity might not exist in Z_n itself, requiring an extension ring. But here's the key insight: for the AKS primality test, if n is composite and the test fails, I output 0 immediately. I only need the DFT to work correctly when n is actually prime, which sidesteps the composite case entirely.

Arakelov geometry and Durov's framework view Spec(Z) as a curve over F_1, where the Riemann zeta function becomes the zeta function of this curve. Let me try a different approach entirely. Instead of computing π(x) directly, what if I work with a related counting function that has better-controlled analytic properties in its Dirichlet series? The von Mangoldt and Möbius functions don't help since their Dirichlet series have the same zeros as ζ(s). But smoothed versions might offer something different.

The existing exact formulas fall into a few categories—sieve methods like Meissel-Lehmer, analytic approaches like Lagarias-Odlyzko, direct enumeration, and the circular "formulas" from Willans and others. Is there a fundamentally different type of formula I'm missing?

What if I approached this through number-theoretic transforms instead? An NTT can multiply polynomials efficiently over modular arithmetic. If I could express π(x) as the output of an NTT-based computation, that might sidestep the Dirichlet series framework entirely. Or maybe through convolution counting—if I define a characteristic function for primes and use multiplicative convolution, I could potentially express π(x) in terms of divisor sums, which might lead somewhere new.

Actually, let me shift to information theory. π(x) contains roughly log(x) bits of information since it's a number around x/log(x). The tricky part is that the oscillatory behavior—the part that's hardest to predict—comes from the zeros of the zeta function, and those positions encode the remaining bits of information.

Each zero at height t contributes oscillations with period roughly 2π/log(t) to the explicit formula for π(x). To pin down π(x) exactly, I need the cumulative effect of all zeros up to height around √x. Since there are roughly √x zeros in that range and each contributes about 1/√x to the sum, the total correction is order 1—but whether it's positive or negative depends on the precise alignment of all those individual terms. The GUE statistics mean these phases are essentially random, so there's no way to predict the sum without knowing every single term, unless there's some hidden global constraint that forces the sum to behave in a particular way.

The Weil explicit formula does provide such a constraint, but it turns out to be circular—it just restates the relationship between the zeros and π(x) without giving me a shortcut. So the fundamental barrier seems real within all known mathematical frameworks. Let me consider some more unconventional approaches: maybe a topological quantum field theory could encode π(x) in the partition function of some carefully constructed 3-manifold, though this is highly speculative and knot invariants have already failed to work. Or perhaps the holographic principle could offer a new angle...

Actually, I'm getting too speculative. Even if I could extract zeta zeros using a quantum computer simulating the Berry-Keating Hamiltonian, the computational cost would be prohibitive for large values. Let me refocus on something more concrete: investigating whether I can compute just the parity of π(x) efficiently, which might be more tractable than the full value.

Previous sessions showed that π(x) mod 2 appears to be just as hard as computing π(x) itself, based on entropy measurements. But I should dig deeper into whether there's a theoretical reason for this. There actually is one—if I could compute π(x) mod 2 efficiently, I could use binary search with the Chinese Remainder Theorem to reconstruct π(x) from its residues modulo small primes. The challenge is that computing π(x) mod p for each prime p seems to carry the same fundamental difficulty.

Let me think about this differently. Computing π(x) mod 2 is really just finding the parity of how many primes exist up to x—it's an XOR operation over all primality indicators. From a circuit complexity perspective, if primality testing is in TC^0, then XOR is too, but the circuit size is still exponential in the input length. This suggests the hardness isn't about the operation itself but about the underlying primality problem. Now I'm wondering if Liouville's function, which tracks the parity of prime factors, might offer a different angle on this.

Liouville's function is completely multiplicative, which gives it a clean Dirichlet series representation. Its partial sums encode information similar to the Möbius function, and there are efficient algorithms to compute them. But I'm not seeing a direct connection between Liouville's function and the parity of π(x)—they're measuring different things. Let me step back and try thinking about this through power towers or iterated exponentials instead.

I'm exploring whether there's a recurrence relation that generates primes efficiently. Mills' theorem shows such constructions exist, but they rely on constants like A that are hard to compute. The challenge is finding a function f where p(n+1) = f(p(1), ..., p(n)) that's both computable and avoids circular dependencies. Most natural candidates either require expensive primality testing or rely on deep analytic functions like zeta evaluations, which defeats the purpose of having a simple recurrence. then "smallest prime > x" can be found by testing x+1, x+2, ... up to x+O(ln^2(x)) (Cramer's conjecture). But the SEARCH takes O(ln^2(x)) primality tests, each O(poly(ln x)), so the total per step is O(polylog(x)).

Wait, this is just the sieve of Eratosthenes in disguise. We'd need to compute p(n) by computing p(1), p(2), ..., p(n-1), p(n), which requires n steps, each O(polylog(p(n))). Total: O(n * polylog(p(n))) = O(n * polylog(n ln n)) = O(n * polylog(n)).

For p(10^100), this is O(10^100 * polylog(10^100)). Way too slow.

The real problem is we're computing all primes up to p(n) instead of finding p(n) directly. I need a better approach. Let me think about whether binary search combined with fast pi(x) evaluation could be optimized differently. Currently binary search on pi(x) costs O(x^{2/3} * log n) since each pi(x) evaluation is O(x^{2/3}) and we need O(log n) evaluations. What if I used Newton's method on the continuous approximation instead? The Riemann R function gives an initial estimate accurate to within O(sqrt(x)/log(x)), so I could evaluate pi(R^{-1}(n)) to check if we've hit the target. done. If not, we know the correction is O(sqrt(x)/log(x)), so we search in a window of that size.

But the window size O(sqrt(x)/log(x)) still contains O(sqrt(x)/log^2(x)) primes, so binary search within the window takes O(log(sqrt(x))) = O(N/2) evaluations of pi(x), each costing O(x^{2/3}). Total: O(N * x^{2/3}).

This is the standard approach and doesn't improve asymptotics.

What if instead of binary search, we use the EXPLICIT FORMULA with a moderate number of zeros to narrow the window?

With T zeros, the error is O(x/T). Using T = x^{1/3} zeros costs O(x^{1/3} * polylog(x)) to compute but only reduces the error to O(x^{2/3}), which doesn't help. If I push to T = x^{2/3} zeros instead, the error drops to O(x^{1/3}), but computing that many zeros via Turing's method costs O(x^{8/9+ε}). Even with the smaller search window of size x^{1/3}, the pi evaluations still dominate at O(x^{2/3} * log(x)), giving a total of O(x^{8/9+ε}), which is worse than the standard approach.

The hybrid approach doesn't work because computing enough zeros to meaningfully reduce the search window is too expensive. Precomputed zeros help only for small arguments—for p(10^100), the error from using just a million precomputed zeros is still O(10^{96}), completely useless.

I'm going in circles with these approaches. Let me pivot to something concrete: can we compute pi(x) using matrix permanents or hafnians?

The permanent encodes a sum over permutations, and if we could construct a matrix whose permanent equals pi(x), we'd have a new angle. But this was already explored and failed—there's no natural way to encode primality into a matrix structure.

Let me try rapidly converging products instead. The Euler product gives us log(zeta(s)) = -sum_p log(1 - p^{-s}) for Re(s) > 1, which relates the prime sum to the zeta function.

For the partial Euler product up to x, Mertens' theorem tells us the convergence rate depends smoothly on prime gaps, so extracting pi(x) from the convergence behavior alone won't work.

Now I'm shifting to a different approach: using the Legendre-counting function phi(x, a), which counts integers up to x not divisible by the first a primes.

The Meissel-Lehmer method expresses pi(x) through phi via a recurrence relation that forms a binary tree, but here's the key insight—while the tree has exponential size, the distinct phi values across different inputs are limited to O(x^{2/3}). If I could memoize this computation as a circuit, I'd precompute all the distinct floor(x/d) values for divisors up to x^{2/3} (roughly O(sqrt(x)) of them) and then apply the Meissel-Lehmer formula using these cached values.

The circuit for computing those floor values has size O(sqrt(x)), and combining them through the Meissel-Lehmer identity gives a tree of logarithmic depth but width O(x^{2/3}), yielding a total circuit size of O(x^{2/3}) or O(2^{2N/3})—still exponential in the bit length N. The real question becomes whether I can compress those O(sqrt(x)) floor values down to something polynomial in N. The floor values {floor(x/k)} for k up to x have exactly 2*floor(sqrt(x)) distinct values, and these generally can't be compressed because they encode arithmetic information about x itself. However, the Meissel-Lehmer method doesn't actually need all of them.

The formula only requires floor(x/d) for a specific set of "special leaves," of which there are O(x^{2/3}/log^2 x). I'm wondering if there's a way to batch-compute the special leaf contributions without evaluating each one individually. The special leaf contribution is a 2D sum over pairs (p,q) where p*q ≤ x and p ≤ x^{1/3}, summing phi(x/(pq), pi(p)-1). I could potentially use FFT or similar techniques on this 2D structure, but the problem is that floor(x/(pq)) doesn't preserve multiplicativity—if x/(pq) were always an integer, I could apply multiplicative FFT, but the floor function breaks that property entirely. This is the same fundamental barrier from before: the floor function prevents convolution-based acceleration.

Now I'm reconsidering whether I need to use the floor function at all. The Legendre sieve counts integers up to x with no prime factors below z by computing an inclusion-exclusion sum with floor terms. But what if I worked with exact real values x/d instead of their floors? Then the formula becomes purely multiplicative and algebraic, which might allow me to factor it differently.

The key insight is that this real-valued version just gives me Mertens' product times x, which is elegant but doesn't actually solve the problem—the hard part is captured entirely in the rounding errors, the fractional parts {x/d}. These fractional parts, weighted by Möbius coefficients, contain all the information needed for the exact count, and understanding their behavior is what analytic number theory really grapples with through techniques like exponential sums and Weyl's method.

The best bounds on sums of fractional parts come from exponential sum estimates, and Huxley's work gives O(N^{2/3}) bounds—which is exactly where the combinatorial sieve hits its wall. So the O(x^{2/3}) barrier isn't some mysterious computational limit; it's directly inherited from the current state of exponential sum technology. The Guth-Maynard result improved zero-density estimates but left the exponential sum bounds untouched, meaning the van der Corput exponent from 2003 still stands as the bottleneck.

Wait, I need to reconsider—the Deleglise-Rivat algorithm actually uses a combinatorial identity rather than exponential sums directly. The O(x^{2/3}) comes from balancing ordinary and special leaves in the Meissel-Lehmer decomposition. But the analytic approach through Lagarias-Odlyzko does rely on zero-density estimates, which connect to exponential sums, and uses Korobov-Vinogradov zero-free regions to get O(x^{1/2+ε}). The Guth-Maynard improvement could tighten that ε, but it won't push past the 1/2 exponent itself.

Let me actually stop theorizing and try something concrete. I want to explore whether we can compute pi(x) using a multiplicative Fourier transform approach. The key insight is that multiplicative characters modulo q give us a Fourier decomposition—we can express the prime count in a specific residue class as a sum involving character sums, and by choosing q as a primorial (product of the first k primes), we get increasingly fine separation between primes and composites. The character sums over primes can then be related to explicit formulas.

The real question is whether this actually helps or just shifts the problem to summing L-function zeros instead. But there's a possibility: if we can find characters where the L-function zero contributions cancel across the family, we might reduce the error to something manageable. This connects to zero-density estimates and the Bombieri-Vinogradov theorem, which gives exactly this kind of averaging behavior. Let me test this numerically by taking q as a product of small primes and computing the character sums over primes up to some bound to see if the cancellation actually materializes. Let me try a parallel binary search approach instead. With multiple processors evaluating pi at different points simultaneously, I can narrow down the search interval faster—after k rounds with P processors, the interval shrinks to (R-L)/(P+1)^k, so reaching size 1 takes roughly log(R-L) / log(P+1) steps, which with polynomial processors gives sublinear parallel time.

But each round still requires computing pi(x), which is exponentially expensive even in the best case. The parallel speedup doesn't matter if the bottleneck is the pi evaluation itself.

I think the real path forward is to run a concrete experiment analyzing the correction term delta(n) = p(n) - R^{-1}(n) through Fourier analysis on different bases, since previous work showed it has about 5 bits of entropy per prime and behaves like a random walk—but I want to probe its structure in a new way.

Actually, let me first check if the background agents have made any progress before diving into new experiments. On second thought, they'll notify me when they're done, so I'll move forward with my own work. I'm considering whether the Mertens function M(x) could serve as a computational proxy for pi(x), since both involve summing over integers up to x but with different indicator functions.

There's a deep connection through the Riemann zeta function and Möbius inversion — the Dirichlet series 1/ζ(s) encodes the Möbius function, and I can potentially use Möbius inversion to relate the prime counting function to M(x). Let me work through the exact relationship more carefully, since the direct formulas get complicated quickly.

Actually, I think the key insight is that Meissel-Lehmer is already optimized for this — it runs fastest when x has many small prime factors because floor(x/d) takes fewer distinct values. So instead of deriving a new formula, maybe I should exploit that by evaluating pi(x) at highly composite numbers where the algorithm naturally becomes more efficient.

But wait, there's a problem with this approach. The gaps between consecutive highly composite numbers grow, so I can't always find one conveniently close to an arbitrary target x. And more fundamentally, Meissel-Lehmer's bottleneck isn't actually x's factorization — it's computing the special leaves, which depends on the primes up to x^{2/3}, not on how smooth x is.

I need to stop circling and commit to something concrete. Let me explore whether the primality sequence could be recognized by automata with more power than finite machines — specifically pushdown automata, which can handle context-free languages. Mauduit-Rivat showed primes aren't k-automatic, but that's just finite automata. If I could show the prime sequence fits into a pushdown automaton or higher in the Chomsky hierarchy, that would place it in a specific computational complexity class. The hierarchy goes: regular languages map to AC^0, context-free to NC^1, and context-sensitive to NSPACE(n).

Since PRIMES is in P via AKS, if I could prove it's in NC^1 or LogCFL, that would be a significant result. But this is getting too theoretical. Let me design an actual experiment using recursive functions instead — specifically, I'll define a recursive counting function for primes up to x based on the smallest prime factors and coprimality with products of initial primes.

The key insight is that this recursion has depth bounded by roughly the square root of x, but the full binary tree would have exponentially many nodes. The real question is whether most of those nodes are actually needed or if the computation can be heavily pruned through memoization. I want to empirically measure how many distinct values are genuinely required versus redundant, what the effective DAG size becomes after memoization, and whether it grows polynomially or exponentially with the input size.

Actually, I realize this is exactly what was already analyzed in the earlier session on the Meissel-Lehmer method—it has O(x^{2/3}) distinct subproblems, which is exponential in log x. So that direction is already settled.

Let me refocus on what's actually unexplored: a completely different mathematical approach to counting primes that avoids the standard techniques like sieve-based floor values, analytic continuation with zeta zeros, or brute-force primality testing. The question becomes whether there's an entirely different computational framework that doesn't rely on any of these conventional methods.

I'm considering several candidates: representation theory of symmetric or general linear groups, modular and automorphic forms that encode prime information through weight and level, geometric approaches using lattice point counting and Ehrhart polynomials, or combinatorial identities that express pi(x) in terms of simpler functions. The lattice point angle seems promising at first—defining primes through divisibility properties—but I need to check whether these formulations are genuinely non-circular or just disguised versions of the same problem.

Representation theory doesn't seem to connect either; the number of irreducible representations of S_n is the partition function, not prime-related. And modular forms have a fundamental limitation: each prime appears as a separate Euler factor, so you can't avoid handling them individually. After exploring these directions, I'm reaching the conclusion that there's no genuinely new mathematical framework for counting primes without essentially relying on the definition of primality itself.

But let me try one more approach with recursive floor sum identities. There's a classical formula involving nested floor functions that might express pi(x), though I'm working through whether it's actually distinct from Wilson's theorem or just a restatement. I'm testing whether certain floor sum combinations can capture primality without circular reasoning.

Actually, I realize Wilson's theorem gives us a direct primality test: (j-1)! ≡ -1 (mod j) exactly when j is prime, so floor(((j-1)!+1)/j) - floor((j-1)!/j) equals 1 for primes and 0 otherwise. The problem is that computing factorials modulo j for each j up to x takes O(x²) time overall, which is slower than the sieve. I'm considering whether maintaining a running product as we iterate could speed this up, but I'm skeptical it avoids the fundamental cost. So maintaining the full factorial isn't practical for large x. Let me shift focus and actually run an experiment to test whether pi(x) can be approximated as a fixed-degree polynomial combination of logarithmic integral evaluations at different roots of x, using optimized coefficients rather than the standard Riemann R(x) formula.
